core_intuitions: Adam's per-coordinate scale invariance: the ratio m_hat/sqrt(v_hat) rescales every coordinate to a near-unit step regardless of the gradient's absolute magnitude, which is why one global learning rate works on transformers' wildly heterogeneous gradients while SGD zig-zags.

The optimizer-state memory tax: AdamW stores two extra fp32 tensors (m, v) plus an fp32 master copy = 12 bytes/param, 3x the bf16 weights (16 bytes/param total). For a 7B model that is ~84 GB of optimizer state alone, dwarfing the 14 GB of weights and motivating the entire second half of the chapter.

The modern optimizers form a spectrum of how aggressively they discard the magnitude of the update while keeping its direction: Adam soft-normalizes each coordinate (~unit), Lion takes the exact per-coordinate sign (+/-1), Muon orthogonalizes a whole matrix so every singular value becomes 1. Lion is the scalar analogue, Muon the matrix analogue of the same idea.

Memory-frugal optimizers cut the '2 buffers per param' cost by different tricks: Adafactor factors the second-moment matrix into a row vector times a column vector (O(n+m) instead of O(nm)); Lion and Muon drop the second moment entirely (1 buffer).

Muon's mechanism: the momentum matrix is effectively low-rank (a few singular directions carry most of the energy). Orthogonalizing it (SVD U-Sigma-V^T -> U-V^T) flattens every singular value to 1, spreading the update energy uniformly across all directions, and this is computed cheaply by a matmul-only Newton-Schulz iteration rather than a real SVD.

Weight decay is not L2 regularization under Adam: L2 folds lambda*theta into the adaptive denominator so high-gradient params get less decay (backwards); AdamW decouples it into a clean multiplicative shrink theta <- (1 - eta*lambda) theta.

new_figures.name: opt-adam-scale-invariance
new_figures.concept: How Adam's m_hat/sqrt(v_hat) turns gradients spanning orders of magnitude into near-uniform unit-size steps per coordinate.
new_figures.rationale: This is the single most foundational and most-interviewed idea in the chapter: WHY Adam is the LLM default over SGD. The prose asserts 'near-unit step regardless of the gradient's absolute scale' but a reader only truly groks it by seeing four coordinates with wildly different raw gradients all emerge with the same step height. Nothing else in the chapter conveys per-coordinate adaptivity as directly.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Adam and AdamW: Derivation, Bias Correction, Decoupled Decay', immediately after the paragraph ending '...that defeat plain SGD.' (the scale-invariance paragraph, ~line 83)
new_figures.brief: A two-panel before/after infographic making Adam's scale invariance visceral. LEFT panel titled 'Raw gradient g' shows 4 vertical bars for 4 example coordinates (label them evocatively: 'embedding row (rare token)', 'LayerNorm gain', 'attention proj', 'MLP proj') with WILDLY different heights spanning roughly 3 orders of magnitude -- draw the tallest bar tall and the shortest a tiny sliver, and annotate each with a small illustrative value like ~1e-4, ~1e-2, ~1e-1, ~1e0 (illustrative only, mark them as such). A single global learning rate arrow across all of them is shown to produce a huge step on one and a negligible step on another -> caption 'one LR cannot serve all: SGD zig-zags'. MIDDLE thin band shows the per-coordinate operation applied to each bar: divide by its own sqrt(v_hat) (the running gradient magnitude) -- draw each bar being scaled by its own little denominator. RIGHT panel titled 'Adam step m_hat / sqrt(v_hat)' shows the SAME 4 coordinates now all at ~equal height (a dashed reference line at 'approx eta / unit step' that all four bars touch). Big takeaway strip along the bottom: 'For a consistent-gradient coordinate m_hat approx sqrt(v_hat), so the step is approx eta -- unit size in every coordinate, independent of gradient scale.' Theme-safe: use neutral/foreground tones plus ONE accent for the 'after' uniformity; no hardcoded reds/greens as sole signal, pair color with the dashed reference line and labels. Keep all four labels legible; bars must not clip the top of the viewBox (leave headroom for the tallest raw bar or use a broken/log-scale hint). Static frame must fully stand alone.

new_figures.name: opt-memory-tax
new_figures.concept: The 16-bytes-per-parameter breakdown of AdamW training memory, highlighting that optimizer state (12 B) is 3x the bf16 weights, with the 7B worked example in GB.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter explicitly calls this 'the systems reality that motivates everything in the rest of the chapter.' The table and worked example are there, but a stacked visual that shows the optimizer slab towering over the weights slab -- and the 84 GB not fitting on an 80 GB H100 -- is what makes the memory pressure (and thus the existence of Adafactor/Lion/Muon/8-bit) click instantly.
new_figures.placement: Section 'The Memory Cost of Optimizer States', immediately after the worked-example admonition (after '...16 versus 8 GPUs.', ~line 186)
new_figures.brief: A single horizontally-stacked bar (or vertical stack) of the 5 memory components for one parameter, each segment width proportional to its bytes, totalling 16 bytes/param. Segments in reading order with byte labels: 'bf16 weights 2', 'bf16 grads 2', 'Adam m (fp32) 4', 'Adam v (fp32) 4', 'fp32 master weights 4'. Group and brace the last three components (m, v, master = 12 B) under a bold bracket labeled 'OPTIMIZER STATE = 12 B/param = 3x the weights'; brace the 2 B weights separately labeled 'actual model'. Use a distinct shade family for the optimizer slab vs the weight/grad slab so the 3x visual dominance is obvious at a glance (theme-safe: differentiate by shade + label + bracket, never color alone). Add a compact side callout titled '7B model' translating each segment to GB (weights 14, grads 14, m 28, v 28, master 28; total 112 GB) with a bold line 'optimizer state alone = 84 GB' and a small annotation 'does not fit one 80 GB H100' with a dashed 80 GB threshold marker crossing the 84 GB bar. All numbers are the chapter's own byte arithmetic and its stated 7B example -- do not invent any others. Everything inside the viewBox; the GB callout must not overlap the byte bar. Legible in both themes.

new_figures.name: opt-discard-magnitude-family
new_figures.concept: The unifying design axis of modern optimizers: Adam (soft per-coordinate normalize) -> Lion (exact per-coordinate sign) -> Muon (per-singular-value orthogonalize), all discarding magnitude and keeping direction.
new_figures.rationale: This is the intellectual spine of the whole modern menagerie and the chapter's most elegant idea, stated as 'Muon normalizes each singular value to 1 the way Lion normalizes each scalar entry to +/-1.' A reader who sees the three side by side finally understands that Lion and Muon are not unrelated tricks but the scalar and matrix versions of one principle. No prose sentence substitutes for seeing them aligned.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Muon: Orthogonalizing the Update', immediately after the paragraph ending '...respects the matrix structure of the weight.' (~line 303)
new_figures.brief: A three-column comparison, one column per optimizer, sharing a common header 'discard magnitude, keep direction'. COLUMN 1 'Adam (soft, per-coordinate)': show a small vector of ~5 coordinate bars of varying heights, then an arrow to the same bars rescaled toward a common ~unit band (dashed 'approx unit' line), caption 'divide each coord by sqrt(v_hat) -> approximately unit'. COLUMN 2 'Lion (sign, per-coordinate)': same input vector of varying-height bars, arrow to all bars snapped to exactly +1 or -1 (uniform height, sign preserved by pointing up/down), caption 'sign(c): every coord = +/- eta, exactly uniform'. COLUMN 3 'Muon (orthogonalize, per-singular-value)': show a MATRIX depicted by its singular-value spectrum -- a little bar chart of singular values sigma_1 > sigma_2 > ... that are spiky/unequal (label 'momentum M = U Sigma V^T'), arrow to a spectrum where every singular value is flattened to 1 (label 'U V^T: all singular values = 1'), caption 'the matrix analogue of Lion's sign'. Along the bottom, a shared axis label reading 'scalar <----> matrix' placing Lion at the scalar end and Muon at the matrix end, with Adam as the soft precursor. Emphasize the parallel visually: coordinate-bars in cols 1-2, singular-value-bars in col 3, same 'spiky -> uniform' motion in all three. Theme-safe neutral palette with one accent for the 'after' uniform state; distinguish up/down sign by direction+label not color alone. Keep three columns balanced and non-overlapping; each caption legible. Must read fully as a static frame.

new_figures.name: opt-muon-newton-schulz
new_figures.concept: Newton-Schulz iteration progressively flattening the momentum matrix's singular-value spectrum toward 1 (orthogonalization) using matmul-only steps, no SVD.
new_figures.rationale: Muon is the chapter's newsmaker and the hardest mechanism to internalize. The key insight -- that a fixed ~5-step cubic matmul iteration drives ALL singular values to 1 -- is inherently a convergence process: a single static frame can show start and end but cannot convey that each iteration nudges the whole spectrum toward the flat line, which is exactly the intuition ('why ~5 steps is enough', 'why it stays matmul-cheap'). Motion earns its keep here.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Muon: Orthogonalizing the Update', immediately after the newton_schulz5 code block (~line 336)
new_figures.brief: An animation of the singular-value spectrum of the momentum matrix converging to the all-ones line over Newton-Schulz iterations. Central panel: a horizontal reference line at value 1 labeled 'target: all singular values = 1'; a set of ~8 vertical bars (or dots on a curve) representing sigma_1..sigma_8. FRAME 0 (X_0 = M / ||M||_F): after Frobenius pre-normalization the spectrum is spiky and mostly BELOW 1 -- a couple of tall-ish leading singular values and a long tail of tiny ones near zero (this depicts the 'effectively low-rank, top few dominate' intuition; annotate 'momentum is low-rank: a few directions carry the energy'). Then step through k = 1,2,3,4,5: on each step the cubic map X <- a*X + (b*A + c*A^2)X pushes the whole spectrum toward 1 -- small values rise, any overshoot settles -- until by ~step 5 all bars sit on the value-1 line (annotate 'singular values -> 1: update is now spectrally uniform'). Show a small caption strip per step: 'iteration k: matmul-only (X X^T then polynomial) -- runs in bf16 at full throughput'. Include a side note that directions U, V^T are unchanged; only the magnitudes (singular values) move. The FINAL static frame (what a non-animated viewer sees) must clearly show the before spectrum (faded/ghosted) and the after flat-at-1 spectrum together with the labels, so the figure stands alone. Use the chapter's coefficients (a,b,c) approx (3.4445, -4.7750, 2.0315) only as a small annotation, not invented numbers on the axis. Theme-safe: one accent for the converged/flat state, neutral for intermediate; label the target line explicitly. Keep bars and captions inside the viewBox; do not let overshoot bars clip the top.

summary: Optimizers is a concept-rich, figure-poor chapter (zero figures) whose intuition rests on four ideas that all reward a picture: (1) Adam's per-coordinate scale invariance, (2) the AdamW memory tax that motivates the entire second half, (3) the unifying magnitude-discard spectrum Adam->Lion->Muon, and (4) Muon's Newton-Schulz orthogonalization. I propose 4 new figures, no refits. Two P1 statics (scale-invariance, memory-tax) carry the core Adam story; one P2 static (the family spectrum) is the elegant spine tying Lion and Muon together; one P2 long-animation (Newton-Schulz) earns motion because singular-value convergence is inherently iterative. I deliberately skip separate figures for the SGD-ravine zig-zag (covered in the earlier calculus chapter) and Adafactor's factorization (adequately served by prose + the memory-tax figure) to stay disciplined.
